1.synthesis: Five days later the high priest Ananias comes down to Caesarea with some elders and a lawyer named Tertullus, who presents the case against Paul to Felix. His speech flatters Felix with thanks for the peace and reforms his foresight has brought (Acts 24:1–3). The charges: Paul is a plague, a mover of dissension among all the Jews throughout the world, and a ringleader of the sect of the Nazarenes; he even tried to profane the temple (Acts 24:5–6). The Jews also join in asserting these things are true (Acts 24:9).
1.historical_context: Tertullus is a professional legal advocate (rētor), apparently a Roman or at least someone skilled in Roman rhetorical convention. His opening praise of Felix (captatio benevolentiae) is exaggerated given what Tacitus and Josephus record of Felix's actual governance—notoriously brutal. The three charges against Paul map onto the Roman legal categories: 'mover of dissension' (seditio—a political crime), 'ringleader of the Nazarenes' (a religious cult charge), and 'tried to profane the temple' (sacrilege—protected by Rome under the ius sacrum). The triple charge structure is designed to invoke the maximum range of Roman law against Paul.
1.literary_structure: The opposing speeches of chapters 24–26 follow the structure of a Roman legal proceeding: prosecution (Tertullus), defense (Paul), delayed verdict. Luke presents both sides' arguments with sufficient detail for readers to evaluate them; the symmetrical structure invites comparison of Tertullus's flattery and Paul's directness. The same charges—political, religious, cultic—are systematically denied in Paul's defense. Tertullus's rhetoric, however polished, cannot produce evidence; Paul's defense, however plain, can produce facts.
1.theological_application: The charge 'a ringleader of the sect of the Nazarenes' is the first time in Acts that the Christian movement is labeled from the outside with reference to Jesus' hometown—a contemptuous designation ('sect of Nazareth'), but one the early believers would accept: they are followers of the Nazarene. The translation of the Jewish religious conflict into Roman legal categories—dissension, cult-leadership, temple desecration—is the strategy of opponents who cannot win a theological argument: reframe the case in categories where they have institutional advantage. The church will face analogous reframings in every era, and Paul's methodical response models the appropriate approach: address each charge directly with the facts.
1.christology: The charge 'ringleader of the sect of the Nazarenes' (Acts 24:5) identifies Paul's movement by its founder's identity: the Nazarene, Jesus of Nazareth, the crucified and risen Lord. Tertullus intends the designation as contemptuous, but it is accurate in the most important way: the movement is defined by its relationship to the person of Jesus. The charges against Paul are ultimately charges about what Jesus is—Messiah, risen Lord, universal savior—reframed in politically exploitable terms. Every legal proceeding against Paul is, at its core, a dispute about Jesus.

10.synthesis: When Felix motions to Paul to speak, he replies: 'Knowing that for many years you have been a judge over this nation, I will cheerfully make my defense' (Acts 24:10). He denies causing any disturbance in the temple—he had gone to worship, not dispute (Acts 24:11–13). He acknowledges following the Way—but also worshiping the God of the fathers, believing everything in the Law and the Prophets, and having the same hope as his accusers in a resurrection of both the just and the unjust (Acts 24:14–15). He came with alms and offerings for his nation; the Asian Jews who should be here are absent (Acts 24:17–19). The only real charge left: 'It is with respect to the resurrection of the dead that I am on trial' (Acts 24:21).
10.historical_context: Paul's polite but not obsequious opening (he notes Felix's experience, not his supposed achievements) contrasts with Tertullus's flattery. Paul's acknowledgment of following 'the Way' and immediately claiming that it is the fulfillment of the Law and Prophets (v.14) is his christological argument in legal dress: Christianity is not a new religion but the fulfillment of Judaism. The mention of 'alms for my nation' (v.17) is Acts' only reference to the Jerusalem collection—introduced with characteristic Lukan understatement. The absence of the Asian Jews who actually made the accusation (v.19) is a significant legal point: the eyewitnesses to the alleged temple desecration are not present.
10.literary_structure: Paul's defense systematically demolishes each charge: no disturbance (counters 'mover of dissension'), the Way is the fulfillment of the Law and Prophets (counters 'sect of Nazarenes' as heresy), no temple desecration (counters sacrilege charge), accusers absent (procedural problem). What remains when all the charges are stripped away: 'the resurrection of the dead' (v.21)—the core theological claim that is not a crime in Roman law. Paul has reduced the legal case to theology, and theology is Felix's problem, not his.
10.theological_application: 'I worship the God of our fathers, believing everything laid down by the Law and by the Prophets, having a hope in God that they themselves accept' (Acts 24:14–15): Paul's self-understanding is continuous with Israel, not discontinuous from it. The Christian claim is not that a new religion has replaced Judaism but that the same God who spoke through the Law and Prophets has now spoken definitively in Jesus, whom Paul proclaims as risen. This continuity argument is both apologetically important and theologically essential: the church's scriptures are Israel's scriptures; its God is Israel's God; its hope is Israel's hope, now specified and secured in the risen Christ.
10.christology: The resurrection of both 'the just and the unjust' (Acts 24:15) is the universal resurrection Paul proclaims—not merely a Christian afterlife but the final judgment of all humanity. This is the hope grounded in Jesus' resurrection: the one who rose is the firstfruits (1 Corinthians 15:20) of a universal resurrection that the Law and Prophets anticipated. Paul's conclusion—'it is with respect to the resurrection of the dead that I am on trial' (v.21)—is the most concentrated christological statement in the Caesarean hearings: everything comes down to whether Jesus rose.

22.synthesis: Felix, having a rather accurate knowledge of the Way, adjourns the case: 'When Lysias the tribune comes down, I will decide your case' (Acts 24:22). He orders Paul kept in custody but with some freedom and friends allowed to attend to his needs (Acts 24:23). After some days Felix and his wife Drusilla, herself Jewish, send for Paul and hear him about faith in Christ Jesus (Acts 24:24). As Paul speaks about righteousness, self-control, and the coming judgment, Felix is alarmed: 'Go away for now; when I get an opportunity I will summon you' (Acts 24:25). He also hopes for money from Paul; he summons him often and converses with him (Acts 24:26). After two years, Felix is succeeded by Porcius Festus; wanting to do the Jews a favor, Felix leaves Paul in prison (Acts 24:27).
22.historical_context: Drusilla was the youngest daughter of Herod Agrippa I—the same Herod who killed James and imprisoned Peter (ch.12)—and had left her husband the king of Emesa to marry Felix; Josephus attributes this to Felix's desire and a magician's persuasion (Antiquities 20.7.2). Felix's knowledge of 'the Way' (v.22) may come from Drusilla's Jewish background. The two-year delay before Felix's replacement by Festus places Paul's Caesarean captivity approximately 58–60 AD. Felix's hope for a bribe (v.26) reveals a corrupt governor exploiting a prisoner—and inadvertently giving Paul two years of relatively comfortable ministry from house arrest.
22.literary_structure: The Felix section closes with the richest portrayal of private encounter with the gospel in Acts: Felix and Drusilla hearing Paul on faith, righteousness, self-control, and judgment. Felix's alarm followed by postponement ('when I get an opportunity') is the paradigmatic response of those who are convicted but not converted—the urgency registered and then deferred. The two years of captivity are a narrative gap that becomes significant: during this time Paul would have had sustained opportunity for the kind of ministry Acts 24:23 hints at ('friends permitted to attend to his needs'). The corrupt political ending—Felix leaves Paul imprisoned to do the Jews a favor—is the ironic reversal of justice.
22.theological_application: Felix's response to Paul's teaching—alarmed at righteousness, self-control, and coming judgment, but postponing indefinitely—is the portrait of a conscience that registers truth and refuses its claims. 'When I get an opportunity I will summon you' is the grammar of perpetual deferral: there is never a good time to act on convictions that require costly change. The church's proclamation of righteousness, self-control, and judgment will always alarm those whose lives are ordered by their opposites; the alarm is the Spirit's work. What the alarmed sinner does with that alarm—whether they act on it or perpetually defer—is the decisive question. Felix's two-year delay is not 'no'—it is the 'not yet' that becomes 'never.'
22.christology: Paul's private discourse to Felix and Drusilla on 'faith in Christ Jesus' (v.24) is the gospel in its most intimate setting: the Roman governor and his Jewish wife hearing about the Messiah from his imprisoned apostle. The content—righteousness, self-control, coming judgment—is the ethical and eschatological framework within which 'faith in Christ Jesus' makes sense: the risen Christ who justifies the ungodly is also the returning judge of all, and the call to self-control is the fruit of genuine faith. Felix's alarm suggests Paul made the connection vividly; his deferral suggests the cost of response was more than he was willing to pay.
